Name: task_specific_model_compression
Title: Task-Specific Rate-Distortion Bounds for Model Compression
Short Hypothesis: The optimal compression rate of machine learning models can be significantly improved by explicitly accounting for task-specific distortion metrics, leading to more efficient model compression strategies.
Related Work: Current literature, such as the works of Isik et al. (2021) and Bu et al. (2019), establishes connections between model compression and information-theoretic principles but largely focuses on general performance metrics. This proposal distinctively emphasizes the necessity of aligning compression strategies with task-specific distortion measures to achieve tighter compression bounds and improved performance.
Abstract: In this proposal, we investigate the role of task-specific distortion metrics in shaping the rate-distortion bounds of model compression strategies for machine learning models. While existing literature has established a foundational understanding of model compression through rate-distortion theory, it often overlooks the impact of specific task-related distortions on achievable compression rates. We hypothesize that by framing model compression as a lossy source coding problem with a defined distortion measure pertinent to the target task, we can derive new, tighter bounds on the achievable compression rate. Our approach will involve a comprehensive analysis of various distortion metrics linked to specific tasks, accompanied by theoretical derivations and empirical validation. The outcomes aim to not only quantify the fundamental limits of model compression but also provide actionable insights for developing more efficient compression algorithms tailored to distinct applications. This research will bridge the gap between theoretical understanding and practical implementation, positioning model compression strategies closer to their operational limits.
Experiments: Evaluate the proposed task-specific distortion measures across various benchmark datasets, including CIFAR-10 and ImageNet, to quantify their effectiveness in guiding model compression.
Derive and validate theoretical bounds on achievable compression rates using different task-specific distortion metrics, and compare these with existing approaches.
Conduct empirical studies on model performance pre and post-compression, measuring accuracy and other relevant metrics to assess the impact of task-specific optimizations.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The effectiveness of task-specific distortion measures may vary significantly across different tasks, potentially limiting generalizability.
The theoretical bounds derived may require stringent assumptions that could be difficult to validate empirically in practice.
Identifying the most appropriate distortion metrics for various tasks may introduce subjective elements that complicate the framework.
